text: The surgeon visualizes the right ureter as it crosses over the right iliac artery during the laparoscopic rectopexy procedure.
The assistant retracts the mesosigmoid ventrally, while the surgeon makes a peritoneal incision over the sacral promontory, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The sacral hollow is clearly exposed, providing a visible area for subsequent mesh fixation during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon employs both blunt and sharp dissection techniques to manipulate and separate tissue layers within the operative field.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the right hypogastric nerve, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon opens the rectovaginal septum, creating a visible separation between the rectum and the vagina.
The surgeon dissects between the rectum and the posterior vaginal wall, separating these structures using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon preserves the lateral ligament during the procedure, aiming to prevent recurrence and reduce the rate of constipation.
The surgeon continues to open the rectovaginal septum broadly, extending the dissection down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision caudally into an inverted J shape, following the right side of the rectum and curving to the left side over the ventral aspect of the rectum and the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon inserts a strip of praline mesh, trimmed to dimensions of three by fifteen centimeters, into the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a temporary hysteropexy by placing transparietal sutures, suspending the uterus to the abdominal wall.
The surgeon uses non-absorbable sutures to attach the praline mesh to the ventral aspect on both sides of the distal rectum, securing the mesh in place.
The mesh is positioned to reinforce the rectovaginal septum, providing structural support to the area.
The surgeon fixes the mesh onto the sacral promontory using a tacker, securing it in place as part of the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon closes the lateral borders of the incised peritoneum over the mesh using resorbable sutures, securing the mesh in place within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon covers the mesh completely with peritoneum to prevent future adhesion of the small bowel to the mesh.
